text: During a thoracoscopic right lower lobectomy, the surgeon identifies and injures an anomalous artery in the inferior pulmonary ligament using an electrical hook. Curved color forceps are applied to control bleeding, followed by the placement of a titanium clip to create space for the endostapler. The vascular bundle is then transected with the endostapler.

During the thoracoscopic rhizopalobectomy for pulmonary adenocarcinoma, the truncus anterior artery is injured by the electrocoagulation hook. Bleeding is controlled with site compression using endoscopic suction, followed by clamping with curved scallop forceps. The superior pulmonary vein is transected for better exposure, and the injured truncus anterior is sutured with side compression using suction. Once bleeding is controlled, the truncus anterior is further dissected and transected using an endostapler.

The surgeon retracts a lymph node tightly adhered to the superior vena cava using a stitch, then performs sharp dissection with scissors. A 5mm cut is accidentally made on the superior vena cava wall, and the suction tip is used to compress the injury. A non-needle holder is used to perform angiography with a 5-0 pulling stitch. Intraoperative pathological assessment of other dissected lymph nodes shows no metastasis, leading to part of the node being left undissected.

During the thoracoscopic red epilobectomy, the exogenous vein is injured by the electrocoagulation hook. The surgeon uses suction teeth introduced through the posterior port to compress the wound and stop bleeding. Further dissection is performed to prepare for suturing. The surgeon uses a routine needle holder with a five-zero purling stitch through the anterior working port to suture the wound, first on one side and then on the other, moving the suction tip to expose the wound and tying the knot.

During the thoracoscopic left upper lobectomy, the surgeon transects the superior pulmonary vein and left upper lobe bronchus. Dense articulations surrounding the pulmonary artery are identified, and the pulmonary artery is carefully dissected to expose the lingual artery.

During the blunt dissection of the lingual artery, an accidental laceration occurs at the junction with the main pulmonary artery, causing massive bleeding that stains the camera view. The surgeon uses a suction tip to compress the wound site for bleeding control, followed by the application of Alice forceps to clamp the vascular wound for further manipulation.

The surgeon dissects the left main pulmonary artery and secures it with an endoscopic atraumatic vascular clamp. Angiography is performed using a running five-zero pulling stitch with an anastopic needle holder through the posterior working port, ensuring the contralateral wall of the artery is not inadvertently sewn.

The surgeon removes the vascular clamp and inspects the area for any leakage. The lingual artery is carefully dissected and transected, followed by the transection of the posterior ascending artery and the oblique fissure.
